The loaves of bread represent Christ as the Bread of life, and the nourishment for the souls of his people. He is the Light of his church, the Light of the world; this light shines in and through his word. By this light we recognize the food prepared for our souls; and we should daily, but especially from Sabbath to Sabbath, feed on it in our hearts with thanksgiving. And just as the loaves were left in the sanctuary, so we should abide with God until he dismisses us.

This offender was the son of an Egyptian father and an Israelitish mother. The mention of his parents shows the common negative effect of mixed marriages. A permanent law for the stoning of blasphemers was established on this occasion. Great emphasis is placed upon this law. It extends to the foreigners among them, as well as to those born in the land. Foreigners, as well as native Israelites, should be entitled to the benefit of the law so as not to suffer wrong; and should be liable to the penalty of this law if they do wrong. If those who profane the name of God escape punishment from people, yet the Lord our God will not allow them to escape his righteous judgments. What animosity against God must be in the heart of man when blasphemies against God proceed out of his mouth. If he who disregarded Moses’ law died without mercy, what punishment will they deserve who despise and abuse the gospel of the Son of God! Let us watch against anger, do no evil, avoid all connections with wicked people, and reverence that holy name which sinners blaspheme.
